# Webanoid game development plan (2026-03-13 13:39Z)

_**User**_

Build a complete browser game called Webanoid, a retro arcade brick-breaker inspired by the 1986 feel of Arkanoid, using Vue 3, Vite, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and Canvas. Do not use React.

This must be developed iteratively so I can watch progress live in the browser while you work.

## Product goal

Create a faithful retro arcade experience first, with subtle web development theming second.

The result should feel like a real 1980s-inspired arcade game:
- fast and readable gameplay
- simple but satisfying visuals
- pixel-era presentation
- strong score-chasing feel
- authentic arcade UI and game flow

Do not turn it into a modern redesign.
Do not use sleek mobile-app styling.
Do not over-theme it into a parody joke game.

## Name and theme

The game must be called Webanoid.

Use a light web development theme in enemies, level art, labels, or power-ups, but keep gameplay and visual tone primarily retro arcade.

Good theme examples:
- broken layout blocks
- corrupted UI components
- popup tiles
- cookie banner segments
- CMS bricks
- fake site-builder blocks
- rogue widgets
- bad plugin fragments

Do not use real brand logos, trademarks, or copied brand assets.
If you need themed visuals, use fictional parody assets that are easy to swap later.

## Tech stack and constraints

Use:
- Vue 3 with Composition API
- Vite
- JavaScript, not TypeScript
- HTML and CSS
- Canvas for gameplay rendering

Architecture:
- Vue should manage menus, overlays, settings, score display, high score entry, and general UI state
- Canvas should handle gameplay rendering
- Keep important game rules in small testable modules or pure functions where practical

Do not use:
- React
- backend services
- heavy dependencies
- unnecessary state libraries
- unnecessary routing
- unnecessary abstractions

Keep the code understandable for a developer familiar with HTML, CSS, JS, PHP, and Vue.

## Development workflow

Work in small milestones.

Execution order:
1. Inspect the current folder
2. Create the Vue + Vite project if needed
3. Install dependencies
4. Set up testing immediately
5. Start the dev server with hot reload as early as possible
6. Confirm the app is running
7. Initialise git if needed
8. Add this remote if needed:
   https://github.com/ashbryant/Arkanoid-style-browser-game
9. Create an initial README
10. Build the game milestone by milestone
11. Run tests after each milestone
12. Do not continue until the current milestone passes tests
13. Commit after each successful milestone
14. Push after each successful milestone when possible

The dev server must stay running once started, unless a restart is required.

Print short status updates before each milestone so I can follow progress.

## Git behaviour

Use git throughout development.

Requirements:
- make small logical commits
- use clear commit messages
- try to push after each milestone

If pushing fails because authentication is unavailable:
- clearly report the exact error
- give the exact git commands I need to run manually
- continue development locally
- keep committing locally
- do not stall the build waiting for git auth

## README requirements

Create README.md early and maintain it throughout development.

Update it after each milestone if anything important changes.

README must include:
- project overview
- design goals
- retro and web-theme direction
- stack used
- install steps
- dev server command
- build command
- test command
- controls
- mobile behaviour
- audio controls
- project structure
- where level data lives
- where assets live
- known limitations
- next improvements

## Code quality rules

Prioritise:
- readable file structure
- small focused modules
- clear naming
- comments on important or non-obvious logic
- comments that explain why, not just what

Do not add useless comments to obvious lines.
Comment important areas such as:
- game loop
- collision logic
- bounce calculations
- input handling
- state transitions
- localStorage score handling
- audio handling
- mobile touch handling

## Testing requirements

Set up best-practice automated tests suitable for a Vue + Vite project.

Use:
- Vitest
- Vue Test Utils where useful

Testing rules:
- write meaningful tests for important logic
- prefer stable tests over brittle tests
- do not fake a passing state with trivial assertions
- do not move on until tests pass for the current milestone

At minimum, test:
- collision detection
- ball bounce angle behaviour
- score updates
- lives handling
- level completion and progression
- high score storage
- important state transitions
- any extracted pure game logic modules

Also add light integration/component tests where practical for:
- title screen
- game state transitions
- mute/settings behaviour
- high score entry flow

After each milestone:
- run tests
- show a short test summary
- only continue once tests pass

Also do a basic browser smoke check after each milestone:
- verify the app still loads
- verify the main feature added in that milestone is visible or usable

## Gameplay requirements

Implement a polished playable brick-breaker with:
- paddle movement left and right
- ball movement and reliable collisions
- wall, paddle, and brick bounce behaviour
- variable bounce angle based on where the ball hits the paddle
- multiple levels
- score system
- lives system
- start screen
- game over screen
- level transition or round transition
- increasing difficulty over time
- win state or difficulty loop
- retro high score table
- arcade-style initials entry for new high scores
- localStorage persistence for top 10 scores

Optional but desirable:
- one simple authentic power-up such as wider paddle, slow ball, or multi-ball

Only add laser if it is implemented cleanly and still feels era-appropriate.

## Audio requirements

Add retro-style sound and music.

Required:
- sound effects for paddle hit, brick hit, losing a life, level clear, game over, and menu confirm/start
- retro-style background music
- master mute toggle
- music on/off toggle
- sound effects on/off toggle if practical

Audio rules:
- keep audio lightweight
- handle browser autoplay restrictions correctly
- only start audio after user interaction if required by the browser
- persist audio preferences locally if practical
- make mute controls visible and easy to use

## Visual style requirements

Visual style must strongly reflect a 1980s arcade feel:
- black or very dark background
- bright pixel-era colours
- chunky UI
- crisp high-contrast playfield
- simple retro title screen
- arcade-style score and status display
- optional subtle CRT/scanline effect if easy to disable

Do not use:
- modern gradients
- glassmorphism
- soft shadow-heavy app styling
- sleek corporate dashboard aesthetics

## Mobile requirements

The game should work on mobile browsers.

Mobile scope:
- support touch input
- use drag or direct touch paddle movement
- prefer landscape play
- show a subtle rotate-device prompt if needed on very small portrait screens
- degrade gracefully on small screens

Do not over-optimise for every device edge case before core gameplay is working.

## Build order

Build in this order:

1. Scaffold project, test setup, dev server, README
2. Title screen and basic retro shell
3. Canvas playfield and render loop
4. Paddle input
5. Ball movement and wall collisions
6. Bricks and brick collisions
7. Score, lives, and state UI
8. Level progression
9. High score persistence and initials entry
10. Mobile touch support
11. Sound, music, and mute controls
12. Web-theme art and content polish
13. Final cleanup, comment review, README review, final test pass

## Working style

Before coding:
- output a short plan
- output the file structure you intend to use

Then work milestone by milestone.

After each milestone:
- explain what was done
- list changed files
- update README if needed
- run tests
- show whether tests passed
- do a browser smoke check
- commit changes
- push if possible

Do not refactor working code unnecessarily.
Do not move to the next milestone if the current one is not working, visible, and passing tests.

## Definition of done

The project is only complete when:
- the game is playable in browser
- the core brick-breaker loop is solid
- the retro presentation is consistent
- high scores are stored locally
- mobile touch controls work
- sound and music work and can be muted
- tests pass
- README is up to date
- code is readable and sensibly commented
- the project runs locally with Vite hot reload

Deliver the project as a complete runnable Vue/Vite game called Webanoid.

Workspace is mostly empty. Creating the project plan and scaffolding the Vue + Vite project.

Plan:
1. Scaffold Vue 3 + Vite project
2. Add Vitest and Vue Test Utils
3. Create project structure for game logic, components, and tests
4. Start dev server for live development

File structure:
```
arkanoid/
├── index.html
├── package.json
├── vite.config.js
├── vitest.config.js
├── README.md
├── public/
├── src/
│   ├── main.js
│   ├── App.vue
│   ├── components/       # Vue UI components
│   ├── game/             # Canvas engine + pure game logic
│   ├── composables/      # useGameState, useAudio
│   └── styles/
└── tests/
```

Scaffolding the project.
